records.ety: [OF. estrangier to remove, F. tranger, L. extraneare to treat as a stranger, from extraneus strange. See Strange.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Estranged (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Estranging.]

defs: To withdraw; to withhold; hence, reflexively, to keep at a distance; to cease to be familiar and friendly with.
quotes: We must estrange our belief from everything which is not clearly and distinctly evidenced.
Had we . . . estranged ourselves from them in things indifferent.

defs: To divert from its original use or purpose, or from its former possessor; to alienate.
quotes: They . . . have estranged this place, and have burned incense in it unto other gods.

defs: To alienate the affections or confidence of; to turn from attachment to enmity or indifference.
quotes: I do not know, to this hour, what it is that has estranged him from me.
He . . . had pretended to be estranged from the Whigs, and had promised to act as a spy upon them.
